one_line_takeaway: Data taggants mark a dataset with clean-label, gradient-matched poison keyed to random out-of-distribution (image, label) pairs, so a model trained on it can be identified from black-box top-k predictions via a rigorous Binomial test -- achieving TPR 1.0 / FPR 0.0 / p<10^-59 at a 0.1% budget on ImageNet1k with no accuracy loss.
key_finding: At a 0.1% budget with matched DeIT-small/3A settings, data taggants achieve perfect detection (TPR 1.0, FPR 0.0) with p < 10^-59 and validation accuracy 64.2 (equal to clean 64.2), while both backdoor-watermarking baselines (Sleeper Agent, BadNets) fail completely (TPR 0.0) and Data Isotopes reaches only TPR 0.53 with a prohibitive FPR 0.20 and degraded accuracy (63.0). Random-pixel keys strongly outperform test-image keys (top-10 key accuracy 87.5% vs 27.5% at 0.1%), justifying the OOD-key design. The mark evades all tested poisoning/anomaly detectors (ROC no better than, and for DBSCAN worse than, random) and remains effective under augmentation and architecture transfer, though confidence drops sharply under mismatch (e.g. Alice SA / Bob 3A: top-10 accuracy falls to 32.5%, log10 p -13.8).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'data taggants', a non-backdoor dataset ownership verification (DOV) technique that lets a dataset owner (Alice) prove, with black-box top-k access only, that another party's (Bob's) model was trained on her dataset. The owner's own contribution (distinct from the cited Witches' Brew poisoning base and prior DOV watermarking work) is: (1) a new detection primitive called 'keys' -- out-of-distribution (pattern, label) pairs where the key image is a uniformly-random-pixel image and the key label is drawn uniformly at random from the label set; the protected dataset is crafted so a model trained on it predicts the key label for the key image; (2) because key labels are random and independent, the null hypothesis (a benign model has top-k accuracy exactly k/|Y| on the keys, Proposition 1) is exactly characterized, enabling a theoretically grounded Binomial significance test with a controlled false-positive rate -- replacing prior work's ungrounded pairwise t-tests / Wilcoxon tests that assume an unjustified threshold on benign-model behavior; (3) the mark is embedded by clean-label targeted data poisoning via per-key parallel gradient matching (aligning gradients of a small perturbed 'signing set' with the key gradient), and stealthiness is improved with an added LPIPS perceptual-loss term. The authors frame the harmless variant of poisoning as 'data signing.'
threat_model: Alice publishes a dataset D_A and suspects Bob will train a model M_B on it without authorization. Alice only has black-box access to M_B, specifically top-k predictions (k small, e.g. k=10), matching restricted-API access. Before publishing, Alice perturbs a tiny signing subset (budget B=0.1% of samples, perturbation bound epsilon=16/255 in L-infinity) and keeps the K keys secret. Detection succeeds if M_B's top-k accuracy on the secret keys exceeds a threshold tau, yielding a low p-value under the Binomial null. Adversary goals/capabilities considered: a dishonest Bob who may (a) try to falsely claim detection (countered by the false-positive guarantee), (b) try to locate/remove the mark via visual inspection, data-poisoning filtering defenses (Deep k-NN, Activation Clustering, Spectral Signatures), or anomaly/OOD detection (DBSCAN), and (c) change architecture, training recipe/augmentations, or train on a superset/subset of Alice's data to evade detection.
limitations: Detection confidence degrades substantially when Bob's architecture or data augmentation differs from Alice's surrogate (worst mismatch cases give weak or inconclusive top-10 signal). A residual (though negligible) validation-accuracy gap versus clean training remains. Stealthiness is imperfect: PSNR is below 30 dB (lower than radioactive data), some taggant patterns are faintly visible, and the authors concede that a resourceful Bob might manually locate clusters of signed images by visual inspection -- left as open work. Most experiments use 100-epoch training rather than the full 800-epoch recipe. The method is designed for image-classification datasets only. The guarantee is a false-positive/statistical certificate, not a robustness guarantee against an adaptive removal adversary.

datasets_used: ImageNet1k (Deng et al. 2009), full and half-class subsets for dataset-change experiments
quantitative_results: Budget B = 0.1% (10^-3) of dataset signed; perturbation bound epsilon = 16/255 (L-infinity); K = 10 keys kept from 20 generated, 8 restarts/key; perceptual-loss weight lambda = 0.01
Detection config: k = 10, tau = 0.1 -> theoretical FPR 0.4%; 4 runs combined via Fisher's method
Table 1 (DeIT-small, 3A, 0.1%): Clean training val acc 64.2+/-0.4; Data taggants val acc 64.2+/-0.6, TPR 1.0, FPR 0.0, log10 p = -59.6
Table 1 baselines: BW Sleeper Agent val 64.4 TPR 0.0 FPR 0.0; BW BadNets val 63.7 TPR 0.0 FPR 0.0; Data isotopes val 63.0 TPR 0.53 FPR 0.20

Table 2 (DeIT-small, 3A, 0.1%): Data taggants top-1 key acc 10.0% (log10 p -4.9), top-10 key acc 87.5+/-5.0% (log10 p -59.6), PSNR 27.9; Transparency top-10 55.0% (log10 p -29.7) PSNR 20.0; Naive Canary top-10 100% (log10 p -74.0)
Table 3 (random vs test-image keys): random-pixel keys at 0.1% give top-10 acc 87.5% (log10 p -59.6) vs test-image keys 27.5% (log10 p -10.4) at comparable PSNR

Robustness (Table 4) to augmentation mismatch: SA/SA top-10 100% (log10 p -74.0); 3A/3A top-10 87.5% (log10 p -59.6); Alice SA/Bob 3A top-10 32.5% (log10 p -13.8); Alice 3A/Bob SA top-10 15.0% (log10 p -1.8)
Clean baseline accuracies (Table 6): ResNet-50 77.9, ResNet-34 74.1, ResNet-18 69.6, DeIT-medium 67.3, DeIT-small(3A) 64.2, DeIT-small(SA) 56.1, DeIT-tiny 53.6
Stealthiness: poisoning defenses (Deep k-NN, Activation Clustering, Spectral Signature) and DBSCAN anomaly detection yield ROC no better than random (DBSCAN worse than random) at detecting taggants
Models trained 100 epochs (vs full 800-epoch DeIT recipe, confirmed in Table 11)
baselines_compared: Backdoor watermarking via Sleeper Agent (clean-label backdoor, Souri et al. 2021)
Backdoor watermarking via BadNets (poisoned-label trigger, Gu et al. 2019)
Naive Canary (raw key copies inserted; topline, non-stealthy)
Transparency (linear interpolation gamma=0.2 of key into signing images)
Test-image keys vs random-pixel keys (ablation of the key source)
Perceptual loss vs weight-decay-on-perturbation for stealth (ablation)
